config.title: Reversing gains: A world with rising hunger
config.abstract: Reversing decades of progress, global hunger is rising again. Escalating conflicts, frequent climate shocks, and fragile food supply chains are driving this reversal, hitting Sub‑Saharan Africa and the Middle East region the hardest.
config.inputs: Bo Pieter Johannes Andree, Bryce Charron, Lorena Corso, Sharad Alan Tandon, Sawaid Abbas, Faisal Mueen Qamer

content.text: Global rise in hunger
Access to sufficient food is a basic human necessity.[reference: fao-foodright] Yet for over 700 million people, this need remains unmet.

Hunger is commonly measured using the prevalence of undernourishment (PoU) estimating the share of people whose usual food intake doesn’t provide enough energy to maintain a normal active, healthy lifestyle.[reference: fao-sdg] Even against this low threshold, almost one in ten people worldwide continue to experience hunger.

Measuring undernourishment, however, is inherently challenging, and few global indicators exist. The prevalence of undernourishment does not rely on household survey data—a common source of development statistics—but instead draws on national accounts. It combines national level information on food production and availability, income levels, and population to produce a rough estimate of inadequate calorie intake.

While this approach has clear limitations and cannot capture individual or household experiences of food insecurity, it remains broadly comparable across countries and over time. As a result, it provides the most consistent long-term global measure of hunger currently available.
After decades of steady progress, hunger has reversed course—rising alarmingly over the last decade.

content.title: Global hunger is on the rise
content.source: Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SN.ITK.DEFC.ZS). Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations ( FAO ), uri: fao.org/faostat/en/#home
content.keyFactLabel: more people faced hunger in 2023, compared to 2015.

scenes.text: Between 2001 and 2015, global hunger fell impressively, nearly halving from 13 percent to 7.5 percent.
Since then, however, progress has stalled and even reversed. By 2023, nearly one in eleven people faced undernourishment.
This pattern is true for nearly all regions. Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, East Asia & Pacific, and Latin America & the Caribbean all saw significant improvements in the first decade since 2000...
…only for gains to slow or reverse.
In South Asia, and Latin America & the Caribbean, hunger rates have risen again…
…while progress in East Asia & Pacific has largely stalled.

The most concerning trend is in the Middle East & North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa where hunger has been on a continuous rise for more than a decade—from 2012 to 2023. In 2023, over 250 million people faced hunger in Sub-Saharan Africa and another 100 million people in the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan region.

content.text: Hunger can have profound and far-reaching consequences for individuals and societies. It undermines physical health, leading to malnutrition, weakened immunity, and, in children, stunted growth, impaired cognitive development, and long-term health complications that can persist into adulthood. It limits educational attainment and future opportunities, perpetuating cycles of poverty across generations.[reference: wb-nutrition] Hunger does not stop at the individual level; it destabilizes communities, entrenches inequality, and accelerates conflict and migration.[reference: wfp-conflict]
Are countries on track to end hunger?

The Sustainable Development Goals set an ambitious target of ending hunger by 2030, measured by the prevalence of undernourishment (target 2.1). Let’s investigate how countries have performed against this target and whether progress to date has been meaningful.

content.title: Over 60 countries are moving further away from ending hunger
content.source: Source of the data. Note: Due to measurement limitations for undernourishment, countries with negligible levels of hunger are assigned a minimum value of 2.5 percent.[reference: wdi-undernourishment]

scenes.text: By the SDGs, 60 countries have already met the target—49 of them before the SDGs began in 2015.
These success stories are concentrated in North America and Europe & Central Asia, where food insecurity is rare.
In contrast, the countries facing the steepest challenges in reaching the target are largely in Sub-Saharan Africa.

Since 2015, prevalence of undernourishment has worsened in 61 countries, mostly concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and the Caribbean regions. Some unexpected examples include Argentina and Peru where prevalence rates are not very high, but the trend in rising hunger is concerning.

In another 12 countries, progress has been slower than expected such as Rwanda, Jamaica, and Indonesia. Expected progress is considered typical progress by a measure described in this note (highlight to be added).

content.text: But why has progress towards ending hunger reversed in recent years? Examining acute food insecurity hotspots and their underlying drivers helps shed light on this question.
Drivers of rising hunger

Several countries are facing alarmingly high levels of hunger. To assess the severity and scale of food insecurity, the Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) was set up. First developed in Somalia in the early 2000s, it is a multi-partner initiative involving governments, humanitarian agencies, and organizations such as the Food and Agriculture Organization and the World Food Programme. The IPC has since become the globally recognized standard for classifying food crises. By creating a shared, evidence-based understanding of crisis severity, it has played a critical role in improving early response and helping avert some of the worst food emergencies seen in the past.

To identify where conditions are most likely to deteriorate, IPC partners also produce the Hunger Hotspots Report—a collaborative early-warning product launched in 2020 and updated every three to four months. The report flags countries at highest risk of acute food insecurity in the near term and highlights the key drivers pushing people toward hunger.[reference: hunger-hotspots25]

content.title: In the last five years, 26 countries have been food insecurity hotspots mostly concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and the Middle East
content.subtitle: Food insecurity hotspots by level of concern and its drivers
content.textParagraph: In the most recent report (June to October 2025), 13 countries are identified as hunger hotspots. Over the last five years, a total of 26 countries have been classified as hunger hotspots at various points in time.
content.visdescription: In the most recent report (June to October 2025), 13 countries are identified as hunger hotspots. Over the last five years, a total of 26 countries have been classified as hunger hotspots at various points in time.
content.keyFactLabel: persons in fragile and conflict affected economies face hunger

content.title: Food insecurity hotspots are emerging due to conflict, climate change, and economic shocks
content.subtitle: Drivers of food insecurity hotspots by country
content.textParagraph: The primary drivers of food insecurity in these countries are often linked to conflict, such as insecurity, displacement, and social instability, as well as climate change factors, including droughts, flooding, and tropical cyclones. Additional contributing factors include the economic impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, which has left many countries struggling to recover from significant job losses.[reference: wdr22] Additionally, rising global food prices have hit hardest in areas reliant on imports due to their lack of local food production [reference: unctad-trade].
content.visdescription: The primary drivers of food insecurity in these countries are often linked to conflict, such as insecurity, displacement, and social instability, as well as climate change factors, including droughts, flooding, and tropical cyclones.

content.text: Let’s first examine how conflict drives hunger.
How conflict drives hunger

Armed conflict is one of the leading drivers of hunger worldwide, with impacts that extend far beyond the battlefield. The prevalence of undernourishment has consistently been higher in fragile and conflict-affected economies than in more stable countries.[reference: wdi-undernourishment]
In fragile and conflict affected economies, about [emphasis: 1 in every 5 persons] face hunger.

content.title: Hunger is rising rapidly in fragile and conflict affected economies
content.textParagraph: During the first decade of the twenty-first century, undernourishment in these regions fell from 24 percent in 2001 to 16 percent in 2011, mirroring global trends. However, since then, hunger has risen faster in the fragile and conflict affected economies than the global average, and by 2022 around 22 percent of people in these countries faced hunger, a striking 11 percentage points higher than the global rate of 9 percent.[reference: wdi-undernourishment]

content.text: Conflict contributes directly to these alarming figures by destabilizing entire food systems, disrupting every stage from agricultural production to the availability of food in markets, and ultimately triggering widespread food insecurity that can engulf entire countries and spill over into neighboring regions.[reference: ifpri-conflictchannels] Food systems are vulnerable to disruption at the following stages:
[emphasis: Food production: ]Conflict often destroys farmland, livestock, and irrigation systems, while displacement forces families to abandon their crops and livelihoods.

[emphasis: Food supply: ]Local markets and supply chains break down as roads are blocked, markets are closed, making food hard to find. With low availability of food in the markets, prices rise sharply, making even basic food unaffordable. In many conflict zones, people are left with few options other than relying on humanitarian assistance.

[emphasis: Economic shocks:] Food insecurity can worsen even without disruptions to food supply. Economic instability—driven by contracting economies, high inflation, or currency devaluation—can push food prices upward, weakening households’ purchasing power and making food harder for households to afford.

[emphasis: Aid delivery:] Conflict also limits the delivery of that aid. Food convoys and humanitarian workers face insecurity, while warring parties may restrict supplies and delivery pathways.[reference: ifpri-conflictchannels]

Finally, such disruptions evidently lead to rising food prices. While price spikes often begin in areas directly affected by violence, their effects ripple across entire economies because food supply chains are deeply interconnected. Myanmar offers a stark example.

content.title: Local violence triggered nationwide food price spikes in Myanmar
content.subtitle: Circles on the map represent violent events with fatalities. The size of the circle represents the number of fatalities
content.events_title: Number of monthly violent events with fatalities

scenes.text: Following the surge in conflict incidents beginning in February 2021, food prices rose sharply and have remained elevated since. According to the World Food Programme, prices have more than quadrupled since the conflict began, deepening food insecurity for millions.

content.text: Some conflicts have impacts that reach far beyond national or even regional borders. The Russian invasion of Ukraine, for instance, disrupted global agricultural exports, including staples such as wheat, maize, and fertilizers. Both countries are among the world’s major grain exporters, and the sudden interruption in supply chains sent global food prices soaring, with low-income and import-dependent countries hit the hardest. The crisis underscored the vulnerability of global food systems to large-scale conflicts, as prices remained volatile and supplies constrained until alternative trade routes and new supply channels were gradually established.[reference: ec-ukraine]

Beyond the immediate crisis, conflict traps communities in a cycle of poverty and food insecurity. Families lose their livelihoods and assets, leaving them unable to recover even after the fighting stops. This makes addressing hunger in conflict-affected regions a humanitarian priority.
How the climate impacts hunger

Food security depends on productive agriculture that can keep up with the growing demand of a rising global population. And farming itself depends on stable climate conditions and is highly sensitive to shifts in temperature, rainfall, and other weather patterns.[reference: epa-ccimpact]

With rising global temperatures and increase in climate-related natural disaster events, agricultural productivity growth is estimated to slow down.[reference: naturecc2021]  The impact on productivity will likely be uneven across countries and increase instability in the global food supply chain. As a result of disruptions to the food supply chain, the food insecurity divide between low- and high-income countries may further deepen.[reference: naturefood2021]

Beyond reducing yields, climate shocks such as droughts, floods, and storms also disrupt food access. Climate change is driving these events to become more frequent and more intense, with severe consequences for food security.[reference: adb-disasters] This point is illustrated by the devastating floods that struck Pakistan’s Sindh region in August 2022—among the worst in the country’s history.

Satellite remote sensing provides a way to assess loss and damage to agriculture. Using the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)—a measure of plant greenness and productivity—researchers can detect changes in cropland biomass. This approach helps quantify the extent and intensity of agricultural losses in disaster-affected areas.[footnote: To be meaningful, NDVI comparisons need to use data from the same part of the growing season. Otherwise, differences may simply reflect seasonal shifts, not real crop damage.]

content.title: Extreme floods damaged more than half of Sindh’s cropland
content.keyFactLabel: Of cropland in Sindh, Pakistan was damaged due to extreme floods

scenes.text: This is the Sindh province in southeastern Pakistan.

One-third of the province is agricultural land, and is central to Pakistan's food supply. The region produces staple cereals such as wheat and rice, industrial crops including cotton and sugarcane, as well as fruit (mango and guava) and horticultural crops (chili, tomato and sunflower).
In August 2021, much of the Sindh area was green. Positive values in NDVI reflect normal vegetation conditions, while negative values correspond to flood-induced damage and reduced greenness.

During the floods in August 2022, an analysis projected severe production losses, including 88 percent (3.1 million bales) of cotton, 80 percent (1.8 million tons) of rice, and 61 percent (10.5 million tons) of sugarcane.[reference: naturesr23]

As a result, [emphasis: the floods affected an estimated 33 million people], or roughly 13 percent of Pakistan’s population[reference: naturesr23] and approximately 57 percent of Sindh’s cropland (large negative NDVI difference compared to 2021), that is 2.8 million out of 4.9 million hectares.

content.text: In such natural disaster events, disruptions can go beyond destruction to agricultural land. Storage and food processing infrastructure can also get damaged, transport routes can be blocked, and as a result disrupt the entire food supply chain. For communities already facing poverty or marginalization, such shocks translate into immediate food insecurity, as households lose both subsistence harvests and market access. [reference: wfpusa]
Fragility in food supply chain

In many countries—particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa—hunger has worsened due to conflict and climate shocks. Yet even where these pressures are present, hunger often remains high for another critical reason: fragile food supply chains. When supply chains are weak, they become the system’s breaking point—easily disrupted by violence, extreme weather, or other shocks—amplifying the effects of crises rather than absorbing them.

Fragility in food supply chains often stems from gaps in transport infrastructure, storage capacity, and access to markets. In large parts of Sub-Saharan Africa, food production is sufficient in aggregate, but poor transport networks prevent food from reaching deficit areas. Nearly one-third of African countries can produce enough food overall, yet struggle to supply remote regions without sharply increasing costs. Food must often travel extremely long distances or pass through a single critical route. Long transport distances raise the risk of spoilage and waste, while reliance on single corridors leaves entire regions vulnerable to disruption when those routes fail.

These challenges are clearly reflected in hunger outcomes across Sub-Saharan Africa. Despite some early progress this century, the prevalence of undernourishment remains alarmingly high. Persistently elevated food prices point to deep structural weaknesses:
Food supply chains in Sub-Saharan Africa are, on average, [emphasis: four times longer] than those in Europe.

These costs associated with food supply chains can account for nearly half the retail price of staple foods such as rice, grains, and cassava. As a result of fragile food supply chains, more than one-third of locally produced food in Sub-Saharan Africa is lost or wasted due to inadequate storage and transport infrastructure [reference: transport-africa].

Sub-Saharan African countries with the highest rates of undernourishment hold the greatest potential for rapid gains. Strengthening food supply chains—through investments in transport, storage, and market access—would reduce losses, stabilize prices, and play a critical role in improving nutrition and broader health outcomes.
Conclusion

Hunger and food insecurity have lasting consequences—particularly on children, whose early deprivation can shape outcomes for generations. Today, the challenge of ending hunger is intensified by conflict, fragile food supply chains, and a changing climate.

Building resilient food systems is therefore essential—not only to withstand immediate shocks but also to protect progress over the long term. Yet action is constrained by gaps in our understanding of food insecurity. Strengthening national statistical systems and expanding household-level data on food consumption and nutrition are critical to identifying vulnerabilities and guiding effective, targeted responses [reference: hh-data].
Ultimately, ending hunger requires protecting the most vulnerable and strengthening food systems to withstand shocks. With resilient food systems, a world free from hunger remains within reach.

references.ipcc2014: 2014a. Climate Change 2014: Impacts, adaptation, and vulnerability. Contribution of Working Group II to the Fifth Assessment Report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (New York, NY, Cambridge University Press).
references.transport-africa: Transport Connectivity for Food Security in Africa: Strengthening Supply Chains
